marketValue.flag: Only one season of WAR -- noisy; treat the surplus as directional.

assetValue.flag: RFA at expiry: team control extends past the contract -- trade value understated; replacement-tier: price is the ledger's median small-deal cost

tradeComps.note: Real trades of players closest to him by PRODUCTION (points rank among same-season peers), age, contract AND years of control — so a controlled scorer matches other controlled scorers, not rentals. The return is only what his old team got back FROM the team that landed him; “package” means he wasn't the only piece going out, so that return isn't his alone. For the genuinely elite — who almost never trade, so their only comps are distressed sales — we show a MODELED estimate of what a player his caliber would command instead.

fairContract.note: Fair AAV = average projected annual market value over the term (aging-curve WAR walk x $4.71M/WAR, each year floored at the league minimum). Longer terms average in decline years, so the fair AAV falls. Real deals carry a term/security premium this does not model -- it is the production-fair anchor, not a prediction of the signing.

value.summary: His total on-ice value, converted to WINS (WAR). It adds up everything he does — even-strength play, the power play and penalty kill, his penalty discipline RELATIVE TO HIS POSITION (defensemen structurally take more than they draw, so only the differential vs other D counts), and faceoffs — and the grade ranks that total against other players at his position over the last three seasons.
value.method: Total WAR sums these four, each priced in wins. The grade is this player's percentile vs same-position peers over the 3-season window.

ppOffense.summary: His impact on the POWER PLAY — how much his team's chances improve with him on the man advantage, graded only against players who actually log power-play minutes (so a bottom-six grinder isn't penalized for never being out there).
ppOffense.method: Power-play expected-goals impact, RAPM-adjusted. Graded vs peers who actually play the PP.

pkDefense.summary: His impact on the PENALTY KILL — how well his team smothers chances while short-handed with him on the ice, graded against other penalty-killers.

finishing.summary: Whether he scores MORE goals than the quality of his shots would predict — pure finishing talent. We take his goals minus the expected goals on every shot of his career, then trust it more the more he's shot (a 2,000-shot sample is real; a 200-shot hot streak isn't yet).
finishing.method: Goals minus expected over career shots, then regressed to the mean by sample size. A high-volume shooter earns a more confident read (higher reliability).

hits60.method: Per-60 rate over 3 seasons (TOI-weighted), ranked vs same-position peers.
hits60.label: Physicality
hits60.summary: Physicality is how often he throws a body check — counted straight from the NHL's play-by-play. He's logged 91 hits over his last 39 games (11.6 per 60 minutes on the ice), and the grade ranks that against other forwards.
hits60.caveat: Home-scorer count -- arena bias is real (some rinks record far more than others). Read big gaps as signal, small ones as noise.

blocks60.method: Per-60 rate over 3 seasons (TOI-weighted), ranked vs same-position peers.
blocks60.label: Shot blocking
blocks60.summary: Shot blocking is how willing he is to get in a shooting lane — counted straight from the NHL's play-by-play. He's logged 25 blocked shots over his last 39 games (3.2 per 60 minutes on the ice), and the grade ranks that against other forwards.
blocks60.caveat: Home-scorer count -- arena bias is real (some rinks record far more than others). Read big gaps as signal, small ones as noise.

takeaways60.method: Per-60 rate over 3 seasons (TOI-weighted), ranked vs same-position peers.
takeaways60.label: Puck stripping
takeaways60.summary: Puck stripping is how often he strips the puck off an opponent — counted straight from the NHL's play-by-play. He's logged 12 takeaways over his last 39 games (1.5 per 60 minutes on the ice), and the grade ranks that against other forwards.
takeaways60.caveat: Home-scorer count -- arena bias is real (some rinks record far more than others). Read big gaps as signal, small ones as noise.

giveaways60.method: Per-60 rate over 3 seasons (TOI-weighted), ranked vs same-position peers.
giveaways60.label: Puck security
giveaways60.summary: Puck security is how often he turns the puck over (fewer is better) — counted straight from the NHL's play-by-play. He's logged 11 giveaways over his last 39 games (1.4 per 60 minutes on the ice), and the grade ranks that against other forwards.
giveaways60.caveat: Home-scorer count -- arena bias is real (some rinks record far more than others). Read big gaps as signal, small ones as noise.

primary_assists60.method: Per-60 rate over 3 seasons (TOI-weighted), ranked vs same-position peers.
primary_assists60.label: Playmaking
primary_assists60.summary: Playmaking is how often he makes the primary pass on a goal — 0.26 per 60 minutes of ice time over his last 3 seasons, ranked against other forwards.

rush_xg60.method: Per-60 rate over 3 seasons (TOI-weighted), ranked vs same-position peers.
rush_xg60.label: Rush offense
rush_xg60.summary: Rush offense is the danger of the chances he creates off the rush — 0.11 per 60 minutes of ice time over his last 3 seasons, ranked against other forwards.

skills.window: recent 3 seasons, TOI-weighted, vs position peers
skills.note: hits/takeaways/giveaways are RTSS-tracked (arena scorer bias, not adjusted); giveaways shown as puck security (100 = fewest). All-situations TOI denominates rates.

edge.note: NHL EDGE puck-and-player tracking; percentiles are league-wide for the season. Tracking starts 2021-22.

shot.note: Raw puck speed (NHL EDGE) -- a physical tool, not a finishing grade.

context.note: From shift overlaps. Headline is usage (ice time of the players around him); production is the model's grade of those players. A big gap means leaned-on help that grades below its minutes.

meta.war: Total WAR = EV (5v5 RAPM) + special teams + penalties + faceoffs + finishing. xG-based play-driving plus shrunk shooting talent; single-season RAPM is noisy -- the pooled multi-year RAPM is the stable read.
meta.rapm: off/def/total RAPM are net xG per 60 at 5v5, teammate- and competition-adjusted (ridge regression over shift stints).
meta.coverage: On-ice metrics cover seasons with shift data: 2010-11 to present (100% of regular-season games).

_explain.summary: Tim Washe's +0.00 total WAR in 2025-26 ranks in the 25th percentile of NHL skaters. It breaks down as: even-strength play (5v5): +0.02 WAR (21st percentile); special teams (PP/PK): +0.04 WAR (73rd percentile); finishing (shooting talent): -0.11 WAR (22nd percentile); penalty discipline (vs position): -0.05 WAR (35th percentile); faceoffs: +0.10 WAR (94th percentile).
_explain.narrative: Tim Washe's +0.00 WAR in 2025-26 is driven most by faceoffs (+0.10, 94th percentile) -- within 5v5 he tilts toward defense (offense -0.08/60, 27th percentile; defense -0.06/60, 29th percentile). His weakest area is finishing (shooting talent) (-0.11, 22nd percentile). (xG-based; single-season RAPM is noisy -- see multi-year RAPM for the stable read.)
